hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Dekho — jab tum apni birthday par school mein toffees baantte ho — ya diwali par mummy boondi ke laddoo banati hain aur ghar ghar batate hain — tab tum sochte ho na ki sabko barabar milen? — Aur agar kuch bach jaayen toh unka kya karein? Yahi toh Euclid's Division Lemma hai! Ye batata hai ki kisi bade number (dividend) ko jab hum kisi chote number (divisor) se divide karte hain, toh humein ek quotient aur ek remainder milta hai, aur remainder hamesha divisor se chota hota hai. Bade numbers ke HCF nikalne mein ye kitna useful hai!

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Bohat se students sochte hain ki har number jiske upar 'root' ka sign laga ho (jaise √4, √9), woh irrational number hi hoga.
hi.correct: Lekin aisa nahi hai! Socho zara — √4 का मतलब क्या है? Woh toh 2 होता है na. Aur 2 ek rational number hai, kyunki tum use 2/1 की तरह लिख सकते हो. Irrational numbers woh hote hain jinhe p/q form mein nahi likha ja sakta, jahan p aur q integers hon aur q ≠ 0, aur jinka decimal expansion non-terminating non-repeating hota hai.

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek kaam karo — apne phone ke calculator par kuch fractions ko divide karke dekho. Pehle 1/2, 3/4, 7/8 jaise numbers ko divide karo. Phir 1/3, 2/7, 5/6 jaise numbers ko divide karo. Dono ke decimal expansions mein kya difference dikhta hai?

en.heading: Example
en.content: Hey, remember how during Diwali, we decorate our homes with those beautiful fairy lights? — Sometimes you have a big string of lights, and you want to arrange them into smaller, equal groups for different parts of the house. Or when you have a big box of sweets, and you want to pack them into smaller, identical boxes for gifts. — The Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic is just like that! It says that every composite number can be broken down into a unique set of prime factors. It's like finding the basic building blocks of any number, which helps us understand them better, like finding the HCF or LCM!

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: Many students think that all numbers they've learned about till now, like 2, -5, 1/2, 0.75, are all rational numbers, and that's it.
en.correct: But no, tum! Real numbers are actually a *big family* that includes *both* rational numbers (like fractions and integers) *and* irrational numbers (like √2 or π). So, a number can't be both rational and irrational at the same time, but it definitely belongs to the bigger family of Real Numbers!

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Ek kaam karo — take about 20-30 small objects like rajma beans, marbles, or even small erasers. Now, try to arrange them into groups of 3, then groups of 5, and then groups of 7. Each time, count how many full groups you made and how many objects were left over. — This simple game shows you exactly how Euclid's Division Lemma works with a dividend, divisor, quotient, and remainder!

hi.heading: उदाहरण
hi.content: Dekho — jab tum apni mummy ke saath ghar ke baahar chhota sa garden banate ho na? Kabhi uski lambaai (length) badhate ho toh kabhi chaudaai (width). Agar tum us garden ka area (क्षेत्रफल) calculate karna chaho, toh woh lambaai aur chaudaai par depend karega. Yahi toh hai polynomial ka ek simple roop — jahan ek value (variable) badalne se poore expression ki value badal jaati hai.

hi.heading: गलतफहमी
hi.wrong: Kabhi-kabhi bachchon ko lagta hai ki √x + 5 ya x + 1/x bhi polynomials hain, kyunki in mein 'x' hai.
hi.correct: Lekin — polynomial mein variable (चर) ki power (घात) hamesha poorn sankhya (whole number) honi chahiye (0, 1, 2, ...). Fraction ya negative powers wale expressions polynomials nahi hote.

hi.heading: गतिविधि
hi.content: Ek graph paper lo. Koi bhi simple linear polynomial choose karo, jaise y = 2x + 4. Ab x ki alag-alag values daalkar y ki values nikaalo aur points plot karo. Dekho — tumhari line x-axis ko kahan touch karti hai?

en.heading: Example
en.content: Dekho — remember when you help your mom make a square-shaped rangoli during Diwali? If you want to know how much space it covers (its area), it depends on the length of its side. If the side is 'x', the area is x². See? That's a polynomial — where changing one value changes the whole thing!

en.heading: Misconception
en.wrong: Many students think that expressions like √x + 3 or x + 1/x are polynomials just because they have the variable 'x' in them.
en.correct: But — for an expression to be a polynomial, the power (exponent) of the variable must always be a non-negative integer (like 0, 1, 2, etc.). So, fractional or negative powers mean it's not a polynomial.

en.heading: Activity
en.content: Ek kaam karo — let's try something fun at home! Take a graph paper and a simple linear polynomial, say y = x - 3. Now, put different values for 'x' and find the corresponding 'y' values. Plot these points on the graph. Where does your line cross the x-axis? That's your polynomial's 'zero'!
